sectionName: Biological and Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage:
A physiology lab examines hemodynamic parameters during the cardiac cycle using a catheterized canine heart preparation. Pressure transducers are placed in the left ventricle, aorta, and left atrium. The resulting pressure tracings are displayed in Figure 1, which shows one complete cardiac cycle lasting approximately 0.8 seconds (heart rate = 75 bpm). Key parameters measured include: left ventricular peak systolic pressure = 120 mmHg, aortic diastolic pressure = 80 mmHg, left atrial mean pressure = 8 mmHg, and left ventricular end-diastolic pressure = 12 mmHg.

The lab also measures stroke volume (SV = 70 mL) and uses it to calculate cardiac output (CO = HR × SV) and ejection fraction (EF = SV/EDV, where EDV = 140 mL). Researchers note that the velocity of blood flow through the aortic valve can be estimated using the simplified Bernoulli equation: ΔP = 4v², where ΔP is the pressure gradient in mmHg and v is velocity in m/s.
questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Left heart pressure tracings during one cardiac cycle. Solid line: left ventricle; dashed line: aorta; dotted line: left atrium. Vertical dashed lines indicate valve opening and closing events.
questions.stem: According to Figure 1, during which phase are both the mitral and aortic valves closed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Isovolumetric contraction — LV pressure is rising but has not yet exceeded aortic pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ejection — blood is being pumped from the ventricle into the aorta

choices.label: C
choices.text: Passive filling — blood flows from the atrium into the ventricle through the open mitral valve

choices.label: D
choices.text: Atrial systole — atrial contraction forces additional blood into the ventricle

explanations.A: Correct. During isovolumetric contraction, the mitral valve has just closed (LV pressure exceeds LA pressure) but the aortic valve has not yet opened (LV pressure has not yet exceeded aortic pressure). With both valves closed, the ventricle contracts against a fixed volume of blood, causing a rapid pressure rise from ~12 to ~80 mmHg. On Figure 1, this corresponds to the steep upstroke between MV closure and AV opening. This phase is truly isovolumetric — no blood enters or leaves the ventricle.
explanations.B: During ejection, the aortic valve is open (LV pressure exceeds aortic pressure), allowing blood to flow from the ventricle into the aorta. The mitral valve remains closed. Since the aortic valve is open, this is not a period when both valves are closed. On Figure 1, ejection corresponds to the plateau where LV and aortic pressures track together.
explanations.C: During passive filling (early to mid diastole), the mitral valve is open (LA pressure exceeds LV pressure), allowing blood to flow from the atrium into the ventricle. The aortic valve is closed. Since the mitral valve is open, both valves are not simultaneously closed during this phase.
explanations.D: During atrial systole, the mitral valve is open to allow atrial contraction to push blood into the ventricle (the 'atrial kick'). The aortic valve is closed. Since the mitral valve is open, both valves are not closed during this phase. Atrial systole produces the 'a wave' visible in the left atrial pressure tracing.

choices.label: A
choices.text: left atrial pressure exceeds left ventricular pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: left ventricular pressure exceeds aortic pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: aortic pressure drops below 80 mmHg during diastole

choices.label: D
choices.text: left ventricular volume reaches its minimum value

explanations.A: When left atrial pressure exceeds left ventricular pressure, the mitral valve opens (not the aortic valve). This occurs during diastole when the ventricle has relaxed sufficiently that atrial pressure pushes the mitral valve open, initiating passive ventricular filling.
explanations.B: Correct. The aortic valve opens when left ventricular pressure rises above aortic diastolic pressure (~80 mmHg in this preparation). On Figure 1, this is the point where the LV pressure curve crosses above the aortic curve during isovolumetric contraction. Once open, blood is ejected into the aorta, and the two pressures track closely together until the end of ejection. The valve is a passive structure — it opens and closes based solely on pressure gradients across its leaflets.
explanations.C: Aortic diastolic pressure of 80 mmHg is the minimum aortic pressure during the cycle, but the aortic valve opening is determined by the pressure gradient between the LV and aorta, not by absolute aortic pressure. The valve opens when LV pressure exceeds whatever the current aortic pressure is at that moment.
explanations.D: Left ventricular minimum volume (end-systolic volume) occurs at the end of ejection, just before the aortic valve closes. The aortic valve opens at the beginning of ejection when the ventricle is near its maximum volume (end-diastolic volume). These are opposite points in the cardiac cycle.

questions.stem: What is the cardiac output and ejection fraction for this heart?

choices.label: A
choices.text: CO = 5.25 L/min, EF = 50%

choices.label: B
choices.text: CO = 5.25 L/min, EF = 70%

choices.label: C
choices.text: CO = 9.80 L/min, EF = 50%

choices.label: D
choices.text: CO = 3.50 L/min, EF = 25%

explanations.A: Correct. CO = HR × SV = 75 beats/min × 70 mL/beat = 5,250 mL/min = 5.25 L/min. EF = SV/EDV = 70/140 = 0.50 = 50%. A CO of 5.25 L/min is within the normal resting range (4–8 L/min), and an EF of 50% is at the lower end of normal (normal ≥ 50–55%). EF is the most clinically important measure of systolic function; values below 40% indicate heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF).
explanations.B: The CO calculation is correct (5.25 L/min), but EF = 70% would require SV/EDV = 0.70, meaning SV = 0.70 × 140 = 98 mL. The stated SV is 70 mL, giving EF = 70/140 = 50%, not 70%. An EF of 70% would be supranormal, seen in young athletes or with inotropic stimulation.
explanations.C: CO = 9.80 L/min would require HR × SV = 9800 mL/min. With HR = 75 and SV = 70: 75 × 70 = 5250, not 9800. A CO near 10 L/min would indicate vigorous exercise or a hyperdynamic state, not a resting preparation.
explanations.D: CO = 3.50 L/min is below normal resting output and does not match the given HR and SV (75 × 70 = 5250, not 3500). EF = 25% would require SV = 0.25 × 140 = 35 mL, indicating severe systolic dysfunction. The stated SV of 70 mL gives EF = 50%.

questions.topic: Cardiac output and ejection fraction calculation

questions.stem: On the aortic pressure tracing in Figure 1, a brief dip occurs immediately after the aortic valve closes. This feature, called the dicrotic notch, results from:

choices.label: A
choices.text: elastic recoil of the aortic wall pushing blood backward against the closed valve leaflets

choices.label: B
choices.text: mitral valve opening creating a sudden pressure drop transmitted to the aorta

choices.label: C
choices.text: left ventricular pressure falling below zero during isovolumetric relaxation

choices.label: D
choices.text: transient backward flow of blood against the closing aortic valve, followed by elastic rebound of the valve leaflets

explanations.A: While aortic elastic recoil does occur (the Windkessel function), it maintains forward flow during diastole rather than pushing blood backward. The dicrotic notch specifically results from the brief reversal of flow that causes the aortic valve to close, not from aortic wall recoil. The recoil occurs continuously during diastole and produces the gradual diastolic pressure decline, not the acute dip.
explanations.B: Mitral valve opening occurs during ventricular diastole but does not transmit pressure changes to the aorta because the closed aortic valve separates the LV from the aorta. Events in the left ventricle during diastole do not directly affect aortic pressure. The dicrotic notch is an intrinsic aortic event related to valve closure dynamics.
explanations.C: Left ventricular pressure does not fall below zero (subatmospheric) during normal isovolumetric relaxation. It drops from systolic levels (~120 mmHg) toward diastolic levels (~8–12 mmHg). Even if it did, the closed aortic valve prevents LV pressure from affecting aortic pressure during this phase.
explanations.D: Correct. As ventricular ejection decelerates and LV pressure begins to fall below aortic pressure, blood momentarily flows backward toward the ventricle. This transient reversal catches the aortic valve leaflets and snaps them shut. The brief backward flow causes a small pressure dip, followed by a slight rebound as the elastic valve leaflets and aortic wall absorb the momentum. This produces the characteristic dicrotic notch — a clinically important landmark indicating aortic valve closure and the beginning of diastole.

questions.topic: Dicrotic notch and aortic valve closure

questions.stem: Using the simplified Bernoulli equation, if the peak pressure gradient across the aortic valve during ejection is 4 mmHg, what is the approximate peak blood velocity through the valve?

explanations.A: If v = 0.5 m/s, then ΔP = 4 × (0.5)² = 4 × 0.25 = 1.0 mmHg. This is below the stated gradient of 4 mmHg, indicating the velocity must be higher.
explanations.B: Correct. Using ΔP = 4v²: 4 = 4v², so v² = 1, giving v = 1.0 m/s. This is within the normal range for peak aortic valve velocity (1.0–1.5 m/s in healthy adults). Higher velocities indicate aortic stenosis — for example, a peak velocity of 4 m/s corresponds to ΔP = 4 × 16 = 64 mmHg, indicating severe stenosis. The simplified Bernoulli equation is widely used in clinical echocardiography to estimate valve gradients from Doppler velocity measurements.
explanations.C: If v = 2.0 m/s, then ΔP = 4 × 4 = 16 mmHg. This exceeds the given gradient of 4 mmHg. A velocity of 2.0 m/s across the aortic valve would suggest mild aortic stenosis (peak gradient 16 mmHg).
explanations.D: If v = 4.0 m/s, then ΔP = 4 × 16 = 64 mmHg, indicating severe aortic stenosis. This is far above the 4 mmHg gradient stated and would represent a pathological condition requiring valve replacement consideration.

questions.topic: Bernoulli equation applied to cardiac hemodynamics

questions.passage:
In a second experiment, the researchers administer pharmacological agents and observe their effects on cardiac function. Agent A is a β₁-adrenergic agonist that increases heart rate (positive chronotropy) and contractility (positive inotropy). Agent B is a muscarinic agonist that decreases heart rate (negative chronotropy) and conduction velocity through the AV node (negative dromotropy). Agent C is a pure vasodilator that reduces systemic vascular resistance (SVR) without directly affecting cardiac contractility.

The researchers measure mean arterial pressure (MAP) using the formula MAP = CO × SVR, where CO = HR × SV. They also note that preload (end-diastolic volume) is determined by venous return, and afterload (the pressure against which the ventricle must eject) is primarily determined by SVR. The Frank-Starling mechanism states that increased preload stretches cardiac myocytes, increasing the force of contraction and stroke volume up to a physiological limit.
questions.stem: After administering Agent A, which of the following changes would be expected?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased heart rate and decreased contractility

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased SVR with no change in heart rate or contractility

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased heart rate, increased contractility, and increased cardiac output

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased preload with decreased ejection fraction

explanations.A: This describes the effects of a parasympathetic (muscarinic) agonist like Agent B, not a β₁-agonist. β₁-adrenergic stimulation increases both heart rate (positive chronotropy via SA node) and contractility (positive inotropy via increased Ca²⁺ release in cardiomyocytes).
explanations.B: This describes Agent C (pure vasodilator). β₁-agonists act directly on the heart, not on peripheral vasculature. While some β-agonists with β₂ activity may cause vasodilation, the passage specifies Agent A is a β₁-agonist, which primarily affects cardiac function.
explanations.C: Correct. Agent A is a β₁-adrenergic agonist, mimicking sympathetic stimulation. β₁ receptors in the SA node increase firing rate (positive chronotropy), while β₁ receptors on ventricular cardiomyocytes increase contractile force (positive inotropy) by enhancing Ca²⁺ influx through L-type channels and increasing sarcoplasmic reticulum Ca²⁺ release. Both increased HR and increased SV contribute to elevated CO (CO = HR × SV). Examples include dobutamine (primarily β₁ inotropy) and isoproterenol (β₁ and β₂).
explanations.D: β₁-agonists increase contractility, which increases ejection fraction (more blood ejected per beat), not decreases it. Enhanced contractility also tends to decrease end-systolic volume, reducing rather than increasing preload for the next beat. Increased preload with decreased EF would suggest heart failure decompensation, not sympathetic stimulation.

questions.stem: Agent C reduces SVR by 40% while CO increases by 20% via the Frank-Starling mechanism. What is the net effect on MAP?

choices.label: A
choices.text: MAP increases by approximately 20%

choices.label: B
choices.text: MAP decreases by approximately 20%

choices.label: C
choices.text: MAP decreases by approximately 28%

choices.label: D
choices.text: MAP remains unchanged because the CO increase compensates for the SVR decrease

explanations.A: An increase in MAP would require the CO change to exceed the SVR change. Since SVR decreases by 40% and CO increases by only 20%, the net product of these changes yields a decrease, not an increase. MAP = CO × SVR, so both factors must be considered multiplicatively.
explanations.B: A 20% decrease would result from simply subtracting the percentages (40% − 20% = 20%), but percentage changes in a product do not subtract linearly. The correct approach is multiplicative: new MAP = (1.20 × CO₀) × (0.60 × SVR₀) = 0.72 × (CO₀ × SVR₀) = 0.72 × MAP₀, a 28% decrease.
explanations.C: Correct. MAP = CO × SVR. If CO increases by 20% (factor 1.20) and SVR decreases by 40% (factor 0.60): new MAP = 1.20 × 0.60 × original MAP = 0.72 × MAP₀. This is a 28% decrease. The vasodilation effect dominates because the 40% SVR reduction is not fully compensated by the 20% CO increase. Clinically, pure vasodilators typically lower blood pressure despite reflex increases in cardiac output.
explanations.D: Complete compensation would require CO to increase by approximately 67% (1/0.60 = 1.667) to offset the 40% SVR drop. A 20% CO increase is insufficient. The Frank-Starling mechanism provides partial compensation — increased venous return from vasodilation stretches the ventricle and increases SV — but cannot fully offset a 40% SVR reduction.

questions.stem: If venous return suddenly increases (e.g., from rapid IV fluid administration), the Frank-Starling mechanism predicts that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: stroke volume increases because greater myocyte stretch produces stronger contraction, without a change in contractility

choices.label: B
choices.text: heart rate increases to accommodate the extra volume while stroke volume remains constant

choices.label: C
choices.text: ejection fraction decreases because the ventricle cannot handle the increased volume

choices.label: D
choices.text: systemic vascular resistance increases reflexively to prevent overfilling of the arterial system

explanations.A: Correct. The Frank-Starling mechanism states that increased venous return raises end-diastolic volume (preload), stretching ventricular myocytes. Greater sarcomere stretch (up to ~2.2 μm optimal length) increases the overlap between actin and myosin filaments and enhances calcium sensitivity of the contractile apparatus, producing a more forceful contraction and increased stroke volume. Crucially, this is an intrinsic property of cardiac muscle — it occurs without changes in autonomic stimulation or contractility (the inotropic state). The heart pumps out whatever volume returns to it, matching output to input.
explanations.B: The Frank-Starling mechanism specifically acts through stroke volume, not heart rate. Heart rate changes are mediated by autonomic nervous system reflexes (sympathetic and parasympathetic), not by the intrinsic stretch-tension relationship of cardiac muscle. While baroreceptor reflexes may secondarily adjust HR, the primary Starling response is increased SV.
explanations.C: In a healthy heart, the Frank-Starling mechanism increases both SV and EDV proportionally (or SV increases more), maintaining or increasing ejection fraction. EF would decrease only in a failing heart that has exceeded its optimal preload range, operating on the descending limb of the Starling curve — a pathological condition, not the normal physiological response.
explanations.D: Increased venous return does not directly trigger increased SVR. In fact, the baroreceptor reflex would detect the increased stroke volume and higher arterial pressure and may decrease SVR to prevent hypertension. SVR adjustments are mediated by arterial baroreceptors and autonomic reflexes, not by the Frank-Starling mechanism.

questions.stem: Agent B (muscarinic agonist) slows conduction through the AV node. Which ECG interval would be most directly prolonged?

choices.label: A
choices.text: QRS complex, reflecting slower ventricular depolarization

choices.label: B
choices.text: ST segment, reflecting prolonged ventricular repolarization

choices.label: C
choices.text: QT interval, reflecting delayed ventricular action potential duration

choices.label: D
choices.text: PR interval, reflecting delayed conduction from atria to ventricles

explanations.A: The QRS complex reflects ventricular depolarization through the His-Purkinje system and ventricular myocardium. The AV node conducts impulses from the atria to the ventricles but does not directly determine ventricular depolarization speed. QRS prolongation results from bundle branch blocks or ventricular conduction abnormalities, not AV nodal slowing.
explanations.B: The ST segment represents the period between ventricular depolarization and repolarization, when the ventricle is uniformly depolarized (the plateau phase of the ventricular action potential). AV nodal conduction speed does not affect this phase, which is determined by ventricular ion channel kinetics (primarily L-type Ca²⁺ and delayed rectifier K⁺ channels).
explanations.C: The QT interval encompasses ventricular depolarization and repolarization (QRS + ST + T wave). While QT prolongation is clinically important (risk of torsades de pointes), it reflects ventricular action potential duration, not AV nodal conduction. Muscarinic agonists acting on the AV node do not directly alter ventricular repolarization.
explanations.D: Correct. The PR interval measures the time from the onset of atrial depolarization (P wave) to the onset of ventricular depolarization (QRS). The AV node is the primary site of delay in this interval, allowing atrial contraction to complete before ventricular contraction begins. Muscarinic (M₂) receptor activation at the AV node opens K_ACh channels and decreases cAMP, slowing conduction velocity and increasing the PR interval. First-degree AV block (PR > 200 ms) can result from excessive parasympathetic tone.

questions.topic: ECG intervals and AV nodal conduction

questions.stem: During isovolumetric relaxation, the left ventricular pressure drops rapidly while volume remains constant. Which cellular event primarily drives this pressure decrease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Closure of voltage-gated Na⁺ channels, terminating the action potential

choices.label: B
choices.text: Active sequestration of cytosolic Ca²⁺ back into the sarcoplasmic reticulum by SERCA, allowing actin-myosin cross-bridges to detach

choices.label: C
choices.text: Opening of ligand-gated Cl⁻ channels, hyperpolarizing the myocyte membrane

choices.label: D
choices.text: Passive diffusion of K⁺ into the myocyte, restoring the resting membrane potential

explanations.A: Na⁺ channel inactivation occurs during phase 1 of the cardiac action potential (early repolarization), well before the plateau phase that maintains contraction. By the time isovolumetric relaxation begins, Na⁺ channels have long been inactivated. The rapid pressure drop during relaxation is driven by the end of mechanical contraction, not by Na⁺ channel closure.
explanations.B: Correct. Ventricular relaxation (lusitropy) is an active, ATP-dependent process driven primarily by SERCA2a (sarco/endoplasmic reticulum Ca²⁺-ATPase). SERCA pumps cytosolic Ca²⁺ back into the SR, lowering [Ca²⁺] below the threshold needed for actin-myosin interaction. As cross-bridges detach, the myocardium relaxes and ventricular pressure falls rapidly. The Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger (NCX) also contributes by extruding Ca²⁺. Impaired SERCA function leads to diastolic dysfunction (heart failure with preserved ejection fraction).
explanations.C: Cardiac myocytes do not express significant ligand-gated Cl⁻ channels involved in relaxation. Cardiac repolarization is driven primarily by outward K⁺ currents (IKr, IKs, IK1), not Cl⁻ channels. More importantly, membrane repolarization alone does not cause mechanical relaxation — Ca²⁺ resequestration is required to physically disengage the contractile apparatus.
explanations.D: K⁺ flows out of the myocyte (not in) during repolarization through delayed rectifier channels, and the inward rectifier IK1 stabilizes the resting potential. However, electrical repolarization precedes mechanical relaxation. The rate-limiting step in pressure decline is the active removal of Ca²⁺ from the cytosol by SERCA, not the passive restoration of membrane potential.
